1:
The chapter opens on a grave, and the witnesses read Samuel's death as the removal of Israel's last steadying hand at the worst possible hour. The grammatical-historical Ellicott gives the great eulogy: since Moses none so great as Samuel had arisen; he had restored the forgotten Law of Moses to the affections of a people almost melted into the surrounding tribes of Canaan, and — his crowning work — founded the prophetic schools that trained Israel's future teachers. He lingers on the phrase that all the Israelites gathered, not one fragment only, as might be feared in that time of division, and, citing Stanley and Josephus, notes that they wept for him not as one mourns a stranger but 'as that in which they were all concerned.' Clarke reckons the prophet about ninety-eight years old, while the Reformed Gill canvasses the Jewish chronology — some months, or more likely a year or two, before Saul — and adds that the Jews kept a fast for him on the twentieth of Ijar.

Over the burial 'in his house' the schools quietly diverge. Clarke takes it of a tomb Samuel had prepared for himself, following the Syriac and Arabic; Ellicott prefers the court or garden attached to the house, since a literal burial within doors would have caused perpetual ceremonial defilement, and he compares Manasseh 'buried in his own house' (2 Chron. 33:20), which 2 Kings 21:18 explains as 'the garden of his own house.' Over the flight to the wilderness of Paran there is a like restraint: Ellicott records the Septuagint's substitution of 'Maon,' meant to keep David's camp nearer Carmel, but judges the change needless, Paran having lent its name to the whole southern desert.

The pastoral weight is carried by the Puritan Matthew Henry and the Wesleyan Wesley, who strike one note: all Israel lamented him, and they had reason, for Samuel had prayed daily for them, and 'those have hard hearts who can bury their faithful ministers with dry eyes.' Henry reads the prophet's retired old age as God's just chastisement of a people who had rejected him, and hears in the loss its sharpest sting — never was there more need of the great intercessor than now, when Saul had grown outrageous and David was driven out. David's withdrawal to Paran Henry takes partly as mourning and partly as prudent fear: bereft of his best earthly friend he judged his danger greater than ever, and it was now, in the tents of Kedar (Ps. 120:5), that the exile sought a wilderness which would remind him of God's old care for Israel. The voices do not contend; together they bury a prophet and set the lonelier stage on which the story of Nabal unfolds.
2:
The witnesses turn from a prophet's grave to a rich man's sheepfolds, and the grammatical-historical commentators first set him in the land. Keil and Delitzsch locate Maon and Carmel in the hill country of Judah (Josh. 15:55) — the present Main and Kurmul, scarcely half an hour apart — and read the whole episode as a window on providence: the story of Nabal's folly and Abigail's wisdom shows 'how Jehovah watched over His servant David,' preserving him from a passionate act that might have endangered his calling to the throne, and turning trouble into a source of prosperity. Ellicott is careful that this Carmel is not the famous northern mountain but the small town near Maon where Saul once set up his monument after the Amalekite war (1 Sam. 15:12); the man was a descendant of Caleb, and even the exact tally of his flocks — three thousand sheep, a thousand goats — is preserved, he suggests, to sharpen the churlishness of the reply that is coming.

The word great becomes the hinge of the moral reading. Keil and Delitzsch gloss it plainly as 'great through property and riches,' and note that the sheep-shearing was regularly kept as a festal meal (Gen. 38:12). The Puritan Matthew Henry turns the same adjective inside out: riches make a man 'look great in the eye of the world,' but to one who measures rightly Nabal 'really looked very mean,' for riches are common blessings that God often gives to Nabals, 'to whom he gives neither wisdom nor grace.'

Henry then sets the two portraits side by side that the chapter will exploit. Nabal's very name signifies a fool, an ill omen his parents should never have hung on him; his wife Abigail — 'the joy of her father' — is a woman of great understanding, yet tied to such a husband because her marriage was arranged with an eye 'more after his wealth than after his wisdom.' 'Wisdom is good with an inheritance,' Henry observes, 'but an inheritance is good for little without wisdom.' The Reformed Gill adds only the topographical note that Nabal lived where his business lay, and Wesley that this was near the desert through which Israel once wandered, a place to stir remembrance of God's care. There is no debate here; the commentators are content to raise the curtain on a wealthy churl and his prudent wife, and to warn how often God trusts great estates to men with no grace to use them.
3:
Here the narrator names his figures, and the witnesses read the names themselves as a summary of two souls. On Nabal they are unanimous. The Reformed Gill, following Kimchi, thinks it a nickname men gave him to fit his conduct; the Wesleyan Clarke derives it from a root meaning to be foolish, base, or villainous, and connects it with the Latin nebulo, a knave; Ellicott links it with naval, to fade away, and treats it as a byword for stubborn folly. Of Abigail — 'whose father is joy' — they are equally agreed, and here Ellicott reaches for Stanley's phrase, calling her 'the good angel of the household' of that ill-starred, boorish chieftain, a woman whose far-sighted piety seems 'far in advance of her age,' a maturity he will later trace to the prophetic schools.

The one real dispute is over the last clause, he was of the house of Caleb. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch and Ellicott keep the received Hebrew Kalibi, 'of the family of Caleb,' as the preferable sense, followed by the Chaldee and Vulgate; Wesley presses it home morally, taking the descent as added 'to aggravate his crime,' that he was a degenerate branch of that noble stock and, like David, of the tribe of Judah. Against this stands an old alternative: the Septuagint, Syriac, and Arabic, deriving the word from kelev, a dog, render it 'a doggish man' — anthropos kynikos, one of a cynical, snarling disposition. Clarke reports this reading with evident relish, and Ellicott notes a further vocalization, K'libi, 'according to his heart.'

Beneath the philology the point is one the whole company feels: character has crept into the very names. Abigail is 'a woman of good understanding, and of a beautiful countenance,' while 'the man was churlish and evil in his doings' — and the commentators cannot help observing how cruelly the two were matched. Clarke says flatly that this woman of sense and beauty was married to the boor 'probably because he was rich,' and 'many women have been thus sacrificed.' Whether we read Calebite or cur, degenerate heir or dog-natured churl, the verse hangs a fool's name and a wise woman's grace over the door before a word of the quarrel is spoken, so that the reader already knows which of the two the story will vindicate.
4:
A single sentence — David heard in the wilderness that Nabal did shear his sheep — draws from the witnesses a picture of David's whole precarious economy in these outlaw years. The grammatical-historical Ellicott makes the most of it, observing that the support of the large band of devoted followers who obeyed David 'must have been usually a very anxious' question. Contributions from the farmers and sheep-masters, he suggests, materially eked out what the men gathered from raids across the Philistine border, and not every such gift will have been given gladly; yet the presence of David's armed company genuinely protected the border land. His position, Ellicott writes in a memorable comparison, 'resembled that of a modern Arab sheik of a friendly Bedaween tribe,' and his career as head of a company of free lances tended, on the whole, to make him popular among the southern tribes of Israel.

This is why the timing matters. The Wesleyan Wesley notes only that shearing times 'were celebrated with feasting,' but that plain fact is the point: a rich man at his sheep-shearing had both plenty to spare and a season of open-handedness, so David's application was neither ill-judged nor unreasonable. The Reformed Gill keeps the reader's eye on the pinch that prompted the message — David 'heard in the wilderness,' and 'a wilderness it was to him, being in want of food,' as the whole relation shows.

Ellicott presses the moral consequence for what follows. Nabal's conduct, he insists, was 'more than churlish and foolish,' precisely because David had on many occasions been of substantial service to the man's own shepherds as they tended their flocks 'in exposed and dangerous localities.' The testimony of those shepherd-folk, offered later in the chapter, may be received as the fair popular estimate of David and his acts through this rough and sorely tried period of his life. So the verse, brief as it is, does double duty: it explains why a future king should be reduced to asking bread of a sheep-master at a feast, and it quietly lays the ground of the just claim that Nabal will so brutally deny. The witnesses do not divide; they simply let the wilderness and the shearing-feast frame the request that is about to be made and spurned.
5:
The verse is all courtesy — David sent out ten young men to Carmel with a greeting in his name — and the witnesses dwell on the honour and the humility folded into it. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch read the whole embassy as a model of Eastern good manners: David bids the men 'greet him in my name,' remind him of the friendly services rendered to his shepherds, and only then solicit a present for himself and his people. The Reformed Gill notes that the very number and quality of the messengers — ten young men — were chosen 'to show honour and reverence to Nabal,' no small deference from one whom Israel already sang of as a slayer of ten thousands.

The Puritan Matthew Henry presses the pathos of it. David, he says, was in such distress that he 'did in effect come a begging to his door,' and it is worth pausing over 'what little reason we have to value the wealth of this world, when so great a churl as Nabal abounds and so great a saint as David suffers want.' Yet David begs like a gentleman. He 'chose a good time to send,' when the shearing feast meant good cheer was stirring and Nabal could not plead that he had nothing to spare. He puts gracious words in the messengers' mouths — 'Go to Nabal, and greet him in my name' — and, most tellingly, bids them call him 'thy son David,' owning the older and wealthier man as a father from whom he might hope for a father's kindness.

Henry finds in this the pattern of a soul that can be brought low without being made bitter. David 'insisted not upon royal dainties,' though he was already anointed; he asked as a favour what he might have demanded as tribute or exacted as a general, and framed the whole approach as the request of a humble well-wisher who had done, and would do, Nabal only good. The commentators raise no quarrel over the verse. Together they hold it up as a lesson that greatness of spirit shows itself as readily in the manner of asking as in the manner of giving, and that David's meekness here will only deepen the guilt of the answer he is about to receive.
6:
The greeting itself — Peace be both to thee, and peace be to thine house, and peace be unto all that thou hast — draws the witnesses to two things: the beauty of the salutation and a real obscurity in its opening words. The Wesleyan Clarke gives the form its full warmth, 'the ancient form of sending greetings to a friend,' invoking prosperity on the man, his household, and all that pertained to him; and he hears in it a gentle hint, that David 'had acted so towards him and his property that nothing had been destroyed, and that all had been protected.' Wesley catches the same double edge: by wishing him continued felicity David 'both congratulates Nabal's felicity, and tacitly minds him of the distress' in which David and his men then were.

The crux lies in the phrase our version renders 'to him that liveth in prosperity.' The grammatical-historical Ellicott lays out the difficulty of the Hebrew lechai: the Vulgate slightly alters the text to read 'to my brother,' the Septuagint offers the impossible 'for seasons,' and it is better, he concludes with Luther, Raschi, and the Talmud, to take it as a popular expression of congratulation not found in the literary language — something like 'may it turn out well,' 'may you be prosperous.' The Reformed Gill weighs the same words differently, hearing either a pointed contrast — Nabal 'liveth in prosperity' while David and his men might rather be said to starve — or a wish 'for life,' that Nabal might enjoy such abundance at next year's shearing as he did now, and all his days.

What none of them misses is the irony the greeting will shortly acquire. The threefold peace, pronounced over a man's person, his house, and his goods, is precisely what David's men had in fact secured for Nabal in the field; and the answer of churlish contempt that meets so gracious a blessing is what makes the scene turn. The debate among the witnesses is narrow and friendly — the exact force of a hard Hebrew idiom — but on the substance they are of one mind: David comes not as a marauder demanding tribute but as a friend pronouncing peace, and the very courtesy of his word measures the rudeness of the reply.
7:
David now states his claim — thy shepherds which were with us, we hurt them not, neither was there ought missing unto them — and the witnesses agree that this is no beggar's flattery but the recital of a genuine debt. The Wesleyan Clarke is emphatic: 'it is most evident that David had a claim upon Nabal, for very essential services performed to his herdmen at Carmel.' David not only did them no hurt and took none of their flocks for his own necessities, but 'protected them from the rapacity of others' — and Clarke sets the scene in living colour, recalling how wandering hordes 'think they have a right to exact contributions of provisions wherever they come,' whereas David 'had done nothing of this kind,' shielding Nabal's men against those who would.

The grammatical-historical Ellicott reads the clause 'neither was there aught missing' as pointing to that same protection, the armed band standing between the herdsmen and 'the frequent raids of the neighbouring people — the Philistines and other more savage and unscrupulous tribes.' He judges the request 'certainly a fair one,' and, quoting Lange and Ewald, notes that apart from the Eastern custom of giving liberally at such merry-makings, David 'had a certain right to ask a gift from Nabal's wealth,' having had 'no small share' in the very security that made the festal joy possible: 'without some part of the superfluity of the inhabitants whom he protected, he could not have maintained himself and his army.'

The Reformed Gill simply marks that David 'observes the favours he and his men had done,' laying the ground of his petition, while the Wesleyan Wesley gives the claim its moral edge: considering 'the licentiousness of soldiers' and the necessities to which David and his men were exposed, such forbearance was 'no small favour, which Nabal was bound both in justice, and gratitude, and prudence to requite.' There is no dispute among them. Together they establish, before Nabal opens his mouth, that what David asks is not charity extorted but kindness owed — that the shepherds were, as the next scene will put it, guarded like a wall — so that the churl's refusal is exposed in advance as an act not merely of stinginess but of flat ingratitude and injustice.
8:
The petition closes with disarming modesty: Ask thy young men, and they will show thee... give, I pray thee, whatsoever cometh to thine hand unto thy servants, and to thy son David. The witnesses admire its reasonableness. The Wesleyan Clarke paraphrases the whole appeal with warmth — since Nabal was making a great feast for his servants, David and his men, 'as having essentially served thee, would naturally come in for a share were we present'; let him therefore send a portion by the ten young men, 'that we also may rejoice with you.' 'Certainly,' Clarke concludes, 'this was a very reasonable and a very modest request,' and he sets beside it an Eastern parallel he has heard among the Hindoos, the suppliant's cry, 'O father, fill the belly of thy son; he is in distress.'

The Wesleyan Wesley fastens on the plea we come in a good day: it was 'a day of feasting and rejoicing, when men are most cheerful and liberal,' when Nabal might relieve them 'out of his abundance without damage to himself,' and when, receiving the mercies of God, he was the more 'obliged to pity and relieve distressed and indigent persons.' The Reformed Gill notes only that the young men Nabal is told to consult are the shepherds who had kept their flocks hard by David's men, the very witnesses who could confirm the claim.

The Puritan Matthew Henry catches the spirit of the phrase 'whatsoever cometh to thine hand.' David, though anointed king, 'insisted not upon royal dainties'; he leaves the amount and the choice entirely to Nabal, content to be thankful for whatever is left over — 'beggars must not be choosers' — and asks not as a debt to be levied by a prince or a general but 'as a boon to a friend.' The twice-repeated deference, 'thy servants' and 'thy son David,' completes the humility of the approach. The commentators find nothing to debate; they simply let the reader see how little was asked, how gracefully, and at how fit a season — so that the enormity of the coming refusal is measured against the smallness and sweetness of the request.
9:
The verse is only a hinge — and when David's young men came, they spake to Nabal according to all those words in the name of David, and ceased — but the witnesses find both a faithful embassy and a small puzzle of translation in it. The Reformed Gill marks the plain fact that the messengers 'came to Carmel' and delivered their charge exactly, keeping strictly to the courteous words David had put in their mouths. That fidelity is itself worth noting: they neither softened nor sharpened the appeal, but laid it before Nabal whole, and then fell silent to await his answer.

The obscurity is in that last word. The grammatical-historical Ellicott observes that 'and ceased' is 'better rendered, and they sat down,' the Hebrew having been variously understood — Bunsen took it as 'they waited modestly for an answer,' the Vulgate and some after it as 'they were silent.' Keil and Delitzsch agree that the proper sense is 'and sat down,' the men settling to wait 'awaiting the fulfilment of their request,' while noting that the Chaldee rendered it 'ceased,' that is, made an end of speaking. The variants converge on a single tableau: ten envoys, their gracious errand fully spoken, seated in expectant quiet before the sheep-master's door. Gill adds that they offered no injury and took nothing by force, but waited to be answered; and the very restraint of armed men who could have seized what they asked only heightens the courtesy of their coming and the outrage of the reply it is about to draw.

That posture of patient waiting is the whole force of the verse, and the commentators let it stand as a held breath. Everything proper has now been done — the greeting of peace, the recital of real service, the modest asking at a generous season, the exact and honourable delivery — and the messengers have nothing left but to wait on the goodwill of the man they have honoured. The witnesses raise no quarrel; the only difference among them is whether the closing verb paints the men as silent, as seated, or as modestly expectant, and on the substance they are one. The stage is set, the request hangs in the air, and the next verse will show what manner of heart the waiting has laid bare.
10:
Nabal's answer — Who is David? and who is the son of Jesse? — is the hinge of the whole chapter, and the witnesses read it as insult heaped on injustice. The Wesleyan Clarke puts the plain moral case: 'it was unjust to refuse so reasonable a request, and the manner of the refusal was highly insulting,' proving true what Nabal's own servants would soon say of him, that he was 'such a son of Belial that one cannot speak to him.' The Reformed Gill hears the very tone — a reply given 'in a very haughty manner, in great wrath, just as churlish covetous persons' answer when they mean to give nothing. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch expose the tactic beneath the scorn: by asking 'who is David?' Nabal means 'what have I to do with David?' and by adding that many runaway servants now break from their masters he deliberately 'set down David as a vagrant who had run away,' the better to justify his own greed.

The Puritan Matthew Henry feels the sting most keenly. The Philistines themselves could own David as 'the king of the land, that slew his ten thousands' (1 Sam. 21:11), yet Nabal, his near neighbour and of his own tribe, 'affects not to know him,' speaking of him 'as an inconsiderable man, obscure, and not to be regarded.' Nabal not only denies the request but upbraids David with his very distress, casting him as a fugitive fitter for the stocks than for kindness — and Henry answers with Isaiah's word that 'the vile person will speak villany' (Isa. 32:5-7), bidding us not think it strange when great merit is thus disgraced.

Ellicott supplies the reading that makes David's coming fury intelligible. The taunt about runaway servants, he argues, 'indicates that Nabal was of the faction of Saul at this time — was reckoned among those who hated David.' In the churlish sheep-master David saw not merely a stingy neighbour but 'a deadly political adversary,' one who with men like Doeg would gladly 'hunt him down like a wild beast'; and without this the vehemence of David's vow would be 'inexplicable.' It is a real difference of emphasis — Clarke and Henry weighing chiefly the ingratitude and rudeness, Ellicott the open declaration of political enmity — but the witnesses converge on one judgement: the refusal was a wanton return of evil for good, and the manner of it a calculated contempt that turned a small denial into a mortal affront.
11:
Nabal presses his refusal into a boast of ownership — Shall I then take my bread, and my water, and my flesh that I have killed for my shearers, and give it unto men, whom I know not whence they be? — and the witnesses divide their attention between the grammar, a famous textual variant, and the sin of the possessive heart. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch note that the Hebrew is cast as a single indignant question, the verb a perfect with vav consecutive, so that the whole runs, 'And I should take my food and my drink and my cattle and give them to men I do not know?' Ellicott hears in 'men whom I know not' the sneer, 'Shall I give largesse to the enemies of my king — to a band of rebel freebooters?'

The mention of water sets off a small controversy. Both Ellicott and the Reformed Gill report that the Septuagint, puzzled that a rich man should reckon water among his goods, replaced it with 'wine'; but both defend the received text. Water, Ellicott insists, 'is a mark of the accuracy of the record,' being exceedingly precious in that arid district — witness Achsah's prayer to her father for springs of water (Josh. 15:19) — though he wryly adds that it can hardly prove the man abstemious, since the same chapter will find him 'prostrate through intoxication.'

The moral is left to the Puritan Matthew Henry, who fastens on the fivefold my. Nabal 'insists much upon the property he had in the provisions of his table,' priding himself that all was his own — and who denied it? Who offered to dispute his title? But he thinks his ownership justifies keeping everything to himself, 'for may he not do what he will with his own?' Henry answers with the whole doctrine of stewardship: 'we mistake if we think we are absolute lords of what we have and may do what we please with it. No, we are but stewards, and must use it as we are directed, remembering it is not our own, but his that entrusted us with it' — for riches, in the Gospel's word, are 'another's' (Luke 16:12). The witnesses do not really contend; the grammar and the variant only frame the one thing they all see, that a churl has mistaken a steward's trust for an owner's licence, and made his very abundance the instrument of his sin.
12:
The messengers turn back empty-handed — they came and told him all those sayings — and the witnesses admire the self-command of men who might well have answered rudeness with violence. The Puritan Matthew Henry praises them warmly: 'they turned their way,' showing their displeasure by breaking off abruptly from such a churl, but 'prudently governed themselves so well as not to render railing for railing,' much less to take by force what should have been freely given; they came and told David, 'that he might do as he thought fit.' In this Henry finds a rule for the Lord's servants when they are abused: they 'must leave it to him to plead his own cause and wait till he appear in it,' as the servant in the parable 'showed his lord' the affronts he had received but did not avenge them (Luke 14:21).

The Reformed Gill reads the same restraint from the men's side: they 'turned their backs on him, without making any reply,' treating him with silent contempt; they neither injured his person nor seized anything by force, but 'were determined to report this usage to David,' not doubting that he would avenge the affront put upon him and them. The temper of the embassy is thus the exact opposite of the master they had approached — patient where he was passionate, silent where he was scornful.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch carry the narrative forward to its consequence, observing that 'the churlish reply could not fail to excite' David's anger, so that he at once commanded his people to gird on the sword and set out with four hundred men to take vengeance, while two hundred remained with the baggage. The verse, brief as it is, marks the turning of the tide: everything to this point has been courteous and patient, and now the report of Nabal's insolence lights the fuse. The witnesses do not divide; together they hold up the messengers' discipline as the foil to what is coming, so that when David flies into a rage his men's earlier calm only sharpens the question the next verses will press — how the servants could bear the affront so meekly, and their master so ill.
13:
David's response is swift and terrible — Gird ye on every man his sword — and here the witnesses part company with their hero. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch state the verdict most fully: however intelligible David's wrath may be in his situation, 'it was not right before God, but a sudden burst of sinful passion, which was unseemly in a servant of God.' By carrying out his intent he 'would have sinned against the Lord and against His people'; and it is the mercy of the story that 'the Lord preserved him from this sin' by sending Abigail across his path just in time. Ellicott agrees on the fact while explaining the heat: 'the formal preparation and the largeness of the force told off for the work showed how terribly David was in earnest,' bent on wiping out the insult in blood — his anger 'quite to be accounted for, though not to be excused.'

The Puritan Matthew Henry presses the astonishment of it, drawing out what David said as the men armed. He 'repented of the kindness he had done,' counting it thrown away on one who 'requited him evil for good'; then he 'determined to destroy Nabal and all that belonged to him,' resolving to cut off every male and spare none. 'Here David did not act like himself,' Henry laments; his resolution was bloody and his oath passionate — 'Is this thy voice, O David? Can the man after God's own heart speak thus unadvisedly with his lips?' The man who but lately spared Saul, who could be 'dumb and deaf' under reproach, is now 'put into such a heat by a few hard words' that nothing but the blood of a whole family will satisfy him.

The Reformed Gill notes only that David was 'provoked to the last degree' to receive so churlish an answer to so humble a message. The witnesses do not divide over the morality — with one voice they call the design a fall — but they weigh its causes differently: Ellicott stressing the real provocation and even the political menace behind Nabal's words, Keil and Henry the sudden mastery of passion over a saint. Henry gathers the lesson they all feel: 'What are the best of men, when God leaves them to themselves, to try them, that they may know what is in their hearts? What need have we to pray, Lord, lead us not into temptation.'
14:
The scene shifts to the household, where one of the young men told Abigail, and the witnesses gather round this nameless servant as the hinge of the deliverance. The Reformed Gill identifies him carefully: not one of David's men but one of Nabal's own shepherds, one of those David had bidden Nabal consult, who had fed the flocks in the wilderness, knew David, and had stood by when the messengers came and were railed on. Ellicott fills in his motive and his wisdom. Accustomed to his master's 'wild and ungovernable' temper, and gauging from the demeanour of David's warlike followers 'how deadly was the insult,' he went straight to his mistress — for Abigail 'had, no doubt, often acted as peace-maker' between her intemperate husband and his neighbours, and with 'a clever woman's wit' she would see at once the grave consequence of words that betrayed 'at once the jealous adherent of Saul and the bitter enemy of the powerful outlaw.'

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch supply the substance of the servant's report and, with it, the theological frame of the whole rescue: Abigail heard 'what had taken place,' and also 'what had been praiseworthy in the behaviour of David's men,' who had done the shepherds no injury but 'were a wall round us by night and by day.' Keil had already read the episode as the record of how the Lord watched over David, and here that watching takes flesh in a servant's timely candour.

The Puritan Matthew Henry draws out the servant's threefold service. He 'did David justice,' commending his men for their civility; he 'did Nabal no wrong,' honestly reporting that his master 'railed on' the messengers and 'flew upon them' in his usual rage; and he 'did Abigail and the whole family a kindness,' making her sensible of the danger, for he was 'very positive evil is determined against our master and all his household.' Henry contrasts the servant's discretion with the folly of speaking to Nabal himself, who would only have flown into a passion. The witnesses raise no dispute; they simply mark how providence often works through a humble, observant servant, and how the wisdom of the household now passes, in a breath, from the man who should have had it to the woman who does.
15:
The servant's testimony to David opens here — the men were very good unto us, and we were not hurt, neither missed we any thing — and the witnesses prize it precisely for the quarter it comes from. The grammatical-historical Ellicott weighs its worth: the speaker was evidently a man 'in high authority in the sheep farms of Nabal,' and his witness, 'occurring as it does in the heart of an episode most discreditable to David, bears weighty testimony to the admirable discipline and the kind forethought of the son of Jesse.' In a season 'when lawlessness and pillage would have been, if not excusable, certainly to be expected,' David had 'so moulded a wild company of free lances' that they were 'positively a blessing to the country, instead of being, as these bands usually have been, a terrible curse.'

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch anchor the report in its plainest sense: the men 'had not only done them no injury, had not robbed them of anything, but had defended them all the while,' being 'a wall of defence against attacks from the Bedouins living in the desert.' The Reformed Gill adds that David's men were 'very kind and civil, yea, very useful and serviceable,' and therefore 'deserved better treatment than they met with from Nabal.'

The significance the witnesses find here runs deeper than a good conduct report. This is Nabal's own household bearing witness for David against Nabal — the very servants he told David to consult (1 Sam. 25:8) now vindicating the claim their master had scorned as a stranger's. The praise is the more telling for being unsought and for cutting across the grain of the moment, when David himself is behaving badly; it is, as Ellicott suggests, 'the popular estimate of David and his acts during this rough and sorely tried period of his life.' The commentators do not divide. They agree that the future king's discipline over lawless men was a real and remarkable thing, that goodness done in secret has a way of finding its witnesses, and that the shepherds' words lay a foundation of simple fact under all the eloquence Abigail is about to build.
16:
The servant's praise rises to its high point in a single image — they were a wall unto us both by night and day, all the while we were with them keeping the sheep — and though only the Reformed Gill comments at length, the witnesses let the metaphor carry the verse. Gill spells out what the wall shut out: David's men protected Nabal's flocks 'against the Philistines, who, as they robbed the threshing floors of Keilah (1 Sam. 23:1), would have plundered the flocks of Nabal'; against 'the incursions of the Arabs in the wilderness,' the posterity of Ishmael who lived by plunder; and even against 'the wild beasts of the desert,' that would else have carried off many a sheep and lamb 'by night or by day.'

The figure of a wall is exact, and the grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch gloss it as 'a firm protection round us,' a bulwark thrown up against the raiders of the wilderness. A wall does not merely refrain from harm; it stands between the sheltered and the danger, silent, constant, and unregarded until the night an enemy comes. That is exactly what David's band had been to Nabal's shepherds — not an occasional courtesy but a settled, round-the-clock guard, so that flocks pastured on the desert's dangerous edge grazed as safely as in a fold.

The weight of the verse lies in its timing and its speaker. It is the second half of a shepherd's sworn account, offered to a mistress in peril and against a churlish master; and the word wall is the plainest possible measure of the debt David asked Nabal to acknowledge. Nabal had answered as though David were a nameless vagrant demanding largesse; his own herdsman answers that David was the very rampart under whose shadow the wealth of the sheep-shearing had been gathered in safety. The commentators find nothing to dispute. They let the image stand as the servant's clinching proof — that the men Nabal scorned had been his flocks' security by day and their watch by night — and as the ground on which Abigail will build her plea, that so real a kindness deserved anything but the return of blood.
17:
The servant ends with a warning and a plea — now therefore know and consider what thou wilt do... for he is such a son of Belial, that a man cannot speak to him — and the witnesses read it as the urgent handing of the household's fate into Abigail's wiser keeping. The Reformed Gill catches the practical thrust: the servant appeals to his mistress to bethink herself 'for the preservation of her family,' since he could do nothing with the master. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch render the whole speech and its ground: 'know and see what thou doest; for evil is determined against our master and all his house' — the same ominous phrase used of Saul's settled malice (1 Sam. 20:9) — 'and he is a wicked man, that one cannot address him.'

On the last words the witnesses pause. Ellicott explains the idiom that brands Nabal past reasoning with: 'a son of Belial.' Belial, he notes, 'was not a proper name, though it subsequently came to be considered one'; it 'signifies simply worthlessness,' so that 'a son of Belial' is merely 'an expression for a bad, worthless fellow.' The phrase is the servant's honest confession that appeal to Nabal is hopeless — one cannot speak to him — and therefore that everything now depends on his mistress acting where he will not be reasoned with.

The verse turns, then, on a single word: consider. The servant does not tell Abigail what to do; he lays the danger before her and trusts her judgement — a quiet tribute to the woman of understanding the narrator introduced at the outset. His warning is exact on every point: the evil is not merely possible but 'determined,' the peril reaches the whole household, and the one man who might avert it by a soft answer is precisely the man who cannot be spoken to. The commentators do not divide; together they show how the crisis has been placed, deliberately and of necessity, in the hands of the household's wisest member, and how the very hopelessness of Nabal — a worthless man past all counsel — becomes the occasion that calls forth Abigail's grace. What she will do with the warning the next verse begins at once to tell.
18:
Abigail acts before she speaks, and the witnesses linger over the lavish, hurried present — two hundred loaves, two bottles of wine, five sheep ready dressed, five measures of parched corn, an hundred clusters of raisins, and two hundred cakes of figs. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch itemize it precisely — the 'roasted grains' familiar from Jesse's provision for his sons (1 Sam. 17:17), the pressed raisin-cakes and fig-cakes — and note that she 'sent these gifts laden upon asses on before her,' following behind to meet David in person, 'without saying a word to her husband.' The Wesleyan Clarke adds the homely detail that the 'bottles' were goat-skins, the bread thin Eastern cakes, and observes shrewdly that the whole was 'a matter of little worth' — 'out of three thousand sheep Nabal could not have missed five' — so that 'had it been granted in the first instance, it would have perfectly satisfied David.'

Over the quantities there is a minor debate. Ellicott records that the Septuagint enlarged the 'five measures' into five ephahs, and Ewald would even change five sheep into five hundred, thinking the provision 'ridiculously small for such an host'; but he judges the plain text right — Abigail 'in her haste, thinking rightly that no time must be lost,' simply prepared 'a liberal present for David's own immediate followers, not for the whole force.' The Wesleyan Wesley defends her disposing of her husband's goods without leave, 'because it was a case of apparent necessity, for the preservation of herself, and husband, and all the family.'

The Puritan Matthew Henry gathers the prudence into a portrait of the virtuous wife. 'A virtuous woman is a crown to her husband, to protect as well as adorn.' Her wisdom appears in her speed — 'she made haste,' for it was no time to trifle when all was in danger — and in her doing it herself rather than trusting a servant. Where Nabal had grudged David even water, she loads the asses with the best the house afforded, knowing that 'a gift pacifieth anger' (Prov. 21:14), as Jacob once pacified Esau. She disposed of Nabal's goods 'not only lawfully, but laudably,' Henry insists, for it was 'for the necessary defence of him and his family, which otherwise would have been inevitably ruined,' husband and wife having 'a joint-interest in their worldly possessions.' The witnesses do not contend; together they set the open hand of the wife against the closed fist of the husband, and let the loaded asses go down the hill as the first, wordless argument for mercy.
19:
Abigail sends the present ahead and keeps her own counsel — Go on before me; behold, I come after you. But she told not her husband Nabal — and the witnesses read both halves as pure prudence. The Reformed Gill explains the order of march: she sent the servants first with the gifts because 'the sight of the present would in a great measure pacify David, and prepare him to hear patiently what she had to say to him.' The strategy is exact — let the loaded asses reach the angry captain before she does, so that generosity has already begun to soften him before a word of pleading is spoken.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch fill in the movement that follows: she followed behind 'to appease his anger by coming to meet him in a friendly manner,' and 'as she rode on the ass' came down 'by a hidden part of the mountain,' where David and his men were descending against her. Keil also marks that the narrator now pauses the action, inserting David's inward vow (1 Sam. 25:21) as a parenthesis to explain the wrath she is riding toward.

The silence toward Nabal the commentators treat as wisdom, not deceit. To have told him would have been useless and dangerous at once — useless, because a drunken and self-willed man could neither counsel nor consent; dangerous, because his churlish temper would only have forbidden the very errand that was to save him. She acts, as Keil notes, for the preservation of the whole house, and acts alone. The verse thus shows the two instincts of a wise mediator working together: she disarms before she reasons, sending mercy on ahead to plead while she comes behind to speak; and she does not squander the precious hours consulting a man past consulting. Gill underlines the shrewdness of the sequence: the gift is to reach David first and begin its work, so that when its mistress arrives she finds a captain already half-softened and 'prepared to hear patiently' the words on which the whole household's life now hangs. The witnesses raise no dispute; they simply admire the quiet generalship of a woman who has measured both the danger before her and the folly behind her, and moves, without noise, to stand between them.
20:
The meeting is staged in a hollow of the hills — as she rode on the ass, she came down by the covert of the hill, and, behold, David and his men came down against her — and the witnesses attend to the topography because the very landscape seems to conspire toward the encounter. The grammatical-historical Ellicott, following Keil, explains 'the covert of the hill' (Hebrew sether hahar) as 'a hidden part of the mountain,' probably 'a hollow between two peaks.' Thus, he observes, each of the advancing parties would 'come down' — Abigail, who approached on one side, and David, who came on the other — and 'would meet in the hollow between.'

The Reformed Gill adds only that she rode down after her servants, so that the present went first into the ravine and its mistress close behind. The picture the commentators draw is almost dramatic in its symmetry: two descents converging blindly on a single sheltered spot, the one party a company of armed and angry men bent on slaughter, the other a lone woman on a donkey bearing food and words of peace, neither seeing the other until the turn of the path brings them face to face.

What the witnesses leave unspoken, the arrangement of the scene makes plain — that the timing is providential. Keil had already read the whole episode as the record of how the Lord watched over David, restraining him from a sin that would have stained his calling; and this concealed meeting-place, where wrath descending and mercy descending are brought together at the exact moment before blood is shed, is the hinge on which that restraint turns. Had Abigail come a little later, or by another road, the four hundred swords would have reached Nabal's gate. Instead the hidden hollow gathers the two streams into one, and the deliverance the servant hoped for begins the instant she 'came down' and 'met them.' The commentators do not divide; they let the geography carry the theology, and mark how the God who governs the hills has appointed the very place where a passionate man will be turned from murder by a wise woman's face.
21:
The narrator now reaches back to show David's mind as Abigail rides toward him — Now David had said, Surely in vain have I kept all that this fellow hath in the wilderness... and he hath requited me evil for good — and the witnesses treat the verse as a deliberate parenthesis baring the passion she must meet. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch mark it as a circumstantial clause 'introduced parenthetically to explain what follows,' and paraphrase its bitterness: 'only for deception have I defended all that belongs to this man in the desert, so that nothing of his was missed, and he hath repaid me evil for good.' Ellicott reads it the same way — these words 'express what David felt,' his 'justification in his own mind for the violent and vengeful act he was about to carry out': Nabal 'had returned indeed evil for good,' repaying long and faithful protection by throwing in David's teeth 'the taunt that he was a rebel and a runaway slave.'

The Reformed Gill notes that David was 'saying' this within himself, or to his men, at the very moment Abigail met him, so that the parenthesis and the encounter fall together — the vow still hot on his lips as the mediator appears.

The witnesses feel the pathos of the complaint even as they will condemn the resolve. There is real wrong in it: David had guarded Nabal's flocks, and Nabal had paid him with insult; the sense of kindness thrown away and gratitude betrayed is one the commentators grant is natural to a wronged man. Henry, on the earlier verses, had voiced the same lament — 'in vain have I kept all that this fellow hath' — and warned that though we be requited evil for good 'we should not repent of the good we have done, nor be backward to do good another time,' since God himself 'is kind to the evil and unthankful.' The verse thus catches David at the exact point where a just grievance is curdling into an unjust revenge, and the witnesses let it stand as the dark backdrop against which Abigail's wisdom, and the oath of the next verse, will be measured.
22:
David's rage crests in an oath — So and more also do God unto the enemies of David, if I leave... any that pisseth against the wall — and here the witnesses are sharpest, both over the ethics of the vow and over a curious turn in its wording. The Wesleyan Clarke refuses all defence of it: 'nothing can justify this part of David's conduct.' He had 'suffered, properly speaking, no wrongs,' and his resolve 'to cut off a whole innocent family' was 'abominable and cruel, not to say diabolic'; whoever would vindicate it 'is, at least constructively, a foe to God and truth' — and Clarke notes that David himself will soon condemn the design and thank God for preventing it (1 Sam. 25:32). The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch read the coarse Hebrew idiom 'any that pisseth against the wall' as meaning simply every male, and note that the oath, unusually, calls down God's punishment not on the swearer but on 'the enemies of David,' comparing Eli's adjuration of Samuel (1 Sam. 3:17).

That odd substitution draws the closest comment. Ellicott observes that the common form is 'God do so to me and more also,' and that here the text reads 'enemies of David' where the sense plainly means David himself; the Syriac and Arabic even smooth it to 'his servant David,' and the Septuagint simply drops the difficult word — but the Hebrew is correct, and 'a superstitious feeling probably lay at the root of this substitution,' the swearer shrinking from cursing his own head. Wesley says the same in brief: 'enemies of David' means 'David himself,' but 'because it might seem ominous to curse himself,' he names his enemies instead.

Beneath the grammar the witnesses press one alarmed question — is this the voice of David? Wesley cries it aloud: 'Has he been so long in the school of affliction, and learned no more patience therein? Lord, what is man!' Ellicott turns it toward the whole matter of rash vows, citing Bishop Wordsworth on 'the non-obligation to keep a solemn oath, taken perhaps in a moment of undue excitement,' with Herod Antipas and the death of the Baptist as the warning example. The real difference among them is one of severity — Clarke condemning the design outright, Ellicott and Keil weighing it as the sinful excess of a genuinely provoked man — but none excuses it, and all agree that the oath David is swearing is one it will be his mercy, not his shame, to break.
23:
Abigail reaches David and throws herself down — she hasted, and lighted off the ass, and fell before David on her face, and bowed herself to the ground — and the witnesses read every motion as calculated grace. The grammatical-historical Ellicott finds in the obeisance, and indeed in 'the whole tone' of her address, the sign of 'her consciousness that she was addressing the anointed of Jehovah, the future king.' Her worst fears were realized when she met him 'accompanied by so large an armed force, evidently bent on some deed of violence'; and she moved at once to 'deprecate his wrath by representing her husband not merely as a bad man, but as one scarcely responsible for his actions.' The Reformed Gill adds that she knew David, or gathered who he was 'by the number of men that followed him,' and so lost not a moment in doing him reverence.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch connect the act to the taut narrative that has led to it: Abigail 'fell upon her face before him, bowed to the ground, and fell at his feet,' and the whole posture is one of a suppliant taking on herself what she hopes David will not avenge. The very haste with which she 'lighted off the ass' is part of the argument, Keil implies: she does not wait to be addressed but flings herself down the instant she sees him, letting speed and lowliness say, before a word, that she comes not to bargain but to sue for mercy.

The Puritan Matthew Henry reads the deep courtesy as itself an argument. Her demeanour 'was very submissive'; she 'put herself into the place and posture of a penitent and of a petitioner, and was not ashamed to do it,' in the sight of her own servants and of David's soldiers, because 'it was for the good of her house.' Henry lays down the maxim the scene embodies — 'yielding pacifies great offences' — for a man in a rage is more surely disarmed by humility than by resistance. The witnesses do not divide; together they show a great lady of the country flinging herself in the dust before a hunted outlaw, and in that very self-abasement beginning to master him, so that the sword raised against her house is checked, before she speaks, by the sight of a wisdom that will not fight but bow.
24:
Her plea begins by taking the whole guilt upon herself — Upon me, my lord, upon me let this iniquity be: and let thine handmaid speak in thine audience — and the witnesses marvel at the boldness and wisdom of the stroke. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch note that she 'takes the guilt upon herself, because she hopes that David will not avenge it upon her,' and that only after this self-offering does she venture to unfold her three-fold argument for pardon. The Reformed Gill pictures her 'as an humble supplicant, having a favour to ask,' fallen at the feet of a captain who was 'probably on foot.'

The Wesleyan Wesley paraphrases the daring substitution: 'Impute Nabal's sin to me, and if thou pleasest, punish it in me, who here offer myself as a sacrifice to thy just indignation.' He then pays her rhetoric the highest tribute, judging that 'this whole speech of Abigail shows great wisdom': by 'an absolute submitting to mercy, without any pretence of justification' — indeed 'rather with aggravation' of the offence — she 'endeavours to work upon David's generosity, to pardon it'; 'there is hardly any head of argument, whence the greatest orator might argue in this case, which she doth not manage to the best advantage.'

The Puritan Matthew Henry weighs the moral character of such intercession. In taking the blame she 'discovered the sincerity and strength of her conjugal affection': 'whatever Nabal was, he was her husband.' Henry sets the two kinds of soul against each other — 'sordid spirits care not how much others suffer for their faults, while generous spirits can be content to suffer for the faults of others' — and finds Abigail among the generous. There is a foreshadowing here that the witnesses feel without forcing: the innocent stepping between the guilty and the avenger, offering to bear what another has earned. The commentators do not contend; together they honour a woman who wins her cause not by defending the indefensible but by shouldering it, and who thereby turns aside a drawn sword with nothing but a bowed head and a borrowed guilt.
25:
Abigail now names the folly she is pleading against — Let not my lord regard this man of Belial, even Nabal: for as his name is, so is he; Nabal is his name, and folly is with him — and the witnesses read it as the delicate art of excusing a fool without defending him. The Reformed Gill and the Wesleyan Wesley both fix on the pun that carries the argument: 'Nabal signifies a fool,' and his wife turns his very name into her plea — the man is what he is called, so his rudeness was folly, not deep malice, and beneath a great man's notice. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch gather her point with Sebastian Schmidt's maxim, 'a wise man should pardon a fool,' and observe that she prays David to 'take no notice of Nabal' before pressing the arguments that follow.

The Puritan Matthew Henry unfolds the whole tact of the sentence. She excuses her husband's fault by 'imputing it to his natural weakness and want of understanding' — 'it is like him; it is not the first time that he has behaved so churlishly; he must be borne with, for it is for want of wit.' The offence 'was owing to his folly, not his malice: he is simple, but not spiteful'; and Henry hears behind her words the very accent of the Gospel — 'Forgive him, for he knows not what he does.' She adds a second, disarming plea, that she herself 'saw not the young men' David sent, or 'they should have had a better answer, and should not have gone without their errand' — intimating that the fool of a husband was at least wise enough to be ruled by his wife.

Henry is honest about the danger in such speech — a wife who blackens her own husband to a stranger might well be blamed — but concludes that she 'said it to excuse his fault and prevent his ruin,' which alters the case entirely. The witnesses do not divide; they admire the fine balance she keeps, owning the whole ugliness of Nabal's conduct while draining it of the venom that would make it worth avenging, so that David is invited not to strike a rival but to overlook a fool. It is, they agree, the wisdom of one who knows that the surest way to save a man is sometimes to confess exactly how little he is worth saving.
26:
Abigail lifts her plea from the man to the providence of God — as the LORD liveth... seeing the LORD hath withholden thee from coming to shed blood, and from avenging thyself with thine own hand — and the witnesses find her boldest and most searching stroke here. The grammatical-historical Ellicott observes that since the oath 'affirmed nothing,' it should be rendered, 'it is the Lord that hath withholden thee' — 'as true as that the Lord liveth, so true is it that the Lord hath withholden thee from coming into blood-guiltiness.' So confident is she 'that she is doing the Lord's work, and that He is standing by her,' that, before the armed band and their angry leader, 'she speaks as though the danger to her husband's house was a thing of the past,' and as though David 'had real cause for thankfulness in that he had been prevented from doing a wanton, wicked act.'

The Reformed Gill reads her certainty as more than tact: it rested on 'an impulse on her own mind' and on what she could already read 'in David's countenance,' a visible softening that told her the providence of God was even then restraining him. When she prays, now let thine enemies, and they that seek evil to my lord, be as Nabal, both Ellicott and the Wesleyan Wesley take it as a dismissal of the churl as beneath David's sword: Nabal is 'too insignificant to be considered,' 'utterly powerless to harm one like David,' and her prayer is that his other enemies may prove 'equally harmless' — as contemptible, and as easily made to yield and beg pardon, as this fool now does by her mouth.

Keil marks that only after this appeal to God's leading, and 'in the third line,' does she at last mention the present, and even then not offer it to David directly but ask that it be given to the young men in his train. The theology the witnesses draw out is exact: Abigail has reframed the whole moment, so that David is no longer a wronged man cheated of revenge but a servant of God graciously kept from bloodguilt. The debate among them is slight — chiefly the grammar of the oath — but on the substance they are one: the truest kindness she does David is to make him see the hand that is holding his own back, and to turn what he might have counted a frustration into a mercy.
27:
Only now, and with studied modesty, does Abigail present her gift — this blessing which thine handmaid hath brought unto my lord, let it even be given unto the young men that follow my lord — and the witnesses dwell on the delicacy of the offering. The grammatical-historical Ellicott notes that 'this blessing' means simply the gift, of which 'Abigail makes little account — it was simply an expression of her homage and good will'; and, in the Eastern manner 'where an inferior approaches a superior,' she brings it in the hand, yet directs it 'not for David' himself but 'for his company,' asking that it be given to his followers. The Reformed Gill hears the same humility, taking the present as one that 'came as a blessing from God, and with good will from her.'

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch weigh the word itself. The Hebrew berakah, 'blessing,' is used of a present here as when Jacob pressed his 'blessing' on Esau (Gen. 33:11), and as Paul names a gift a 'blessing' (2 Cor. 9:5); and she asks that it be laid, literally, 'at the feet of' the young men in David's train. The Wesleyan Wesley draws the sense out: a gift is called a 'blessing' both 'because the matter of it comes from God's blessing' and 'because it is given with a blessing, or with a good will'; and she offers it to the young men 'as being unworthy of thine acceptance or use.'

The point the witnesses find here is the fine grace of the manoeuvre. To have pressed the present on David directly might have looked like a bribe, or like a claim on his notice; to route it to his followers turns it into a courtesy, an act of homage that binds without buying. It is of a piece with all her wisdom in this scene — she takes the blame she does not owe, excuses the fool she cannot defend, ascribes David's restraint to God, and now gives lavishly while professing her gift too poor for his hand. The commentators raise no dispute; together they show a woman who understands that generosity, like humility, is most disarming when it asks nothing and claims nothing, and who lays her 'blessing' at the feet of the very men her husband had sent away with insults.
28:
Abigail moves from pardon to prophecy — forgive the trespass of thine handmaid: for the LORD will certainly make my lord a sure house; because my lord fighteth the battles of the LORD — and the witnesses hear her rise, almost unawares, into the language of inspiration. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch say her words 'almost amount to prophecy,' so clearly do they express 'her firm belief in the divine election of David as king': the Lord will build him 'a lasting house,' the very promise God will later confirm by Nathan (1 Sam. 2:35; 2 Sam. 7:8), for 'my lord fighteth the wars of Jehovah' (1 Sam. 18:17), and no evil — that is, no lasting misfortune — will be found in him all his days. Ellicott takes the 'sure house' further still: Abigail 'unconsciously' repeats words she may have heard 'in Samuel's Naioth by Ramah,' words the Eternal would make good far down the ages in that greater Son whose throne is sure for ever.

On the phrase the battles of the LORD Ellicott dwells with delight. Pious Israelites of her time reckoned the nation's wars against the idolatrous peoples as Jehovah's own — there was even a lost 'Book of the Wars of the Lord' (Num. 21:14) — and since his combat with Goliath, David 'had been the popular hero and the favourite subject of those folk-songs' that loved to sing of those wars. The Wesleyan voices turn the same clause into counsel. Wesley bids David let this be his 'proper work,' warning that 'it is not thy work to draw thy sword in thy own private quarrel against any of the people of the Lord'; and both he and Clarke take 'evil hath not been found in thee' to mean that David 'hast not committed any act of this kind hitherto,' so that he should not now, by cruelty, 'justify thine enemies' reproaches.'

The Reformed Gill notes only that she asks forgiveness of 'the trespass' — whether Nabal's sin, which she has made her own, or her own boldness in pressing the suit. But the force the witnesses feel is cumulative. Abigail has bound the appeal for pardon to a vision of David's future: because God means to make him king and a lasting house, he must not stain that calling with private blood. Keil grounds her astonishing certainty in real knowledge, most likely from Samuel or the prophetic schools; Ellicott hears in it a note that overleaps her own understanding. Either way, the commentators agree, her plea has become a sermon — that the man whom God has marked for a throne is exactly the man who must keep his hands clean of vengeance.
29:
Abigail's speech reaches its lyrical summit — the soul of my lord shall be bound in the bundle of life with the LORD thy God; and the souls of thine enemies, them shall he sling out, as out of the middle of a sling — and the witnesses treat the image with something like reverence. All note her courtesy in the opening clause: when she says a man is risen to pursue thee, she means Saul, but, as Ellicott and the Wesleyan Wesley and the Reformed Gill all observe, she 'refrains from mentioning in connection with evil the name of her king, the anointed of Jehovah.' The metaphor of the bundle, the grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch explain, is 'taken from the custom of binding up valuable things in a bundle, to prevent their being injured'; Clarke hears the promise that David's life is 'precious in the sight of the Lord,' supported by 'the Fountain of life,' while his enemies are 'cast off from himself as a stone is cast out from a sling,' with force and violence.

How far the saying reaches is the one real question. Keil and Delitzsch insist the words 'do not refer primarily to eternal life with God in heaven, but only to the safe preservation of the righteous on this earth' — yet add that whoever is so hidden in God's fellowship that no enemy can harm him, 'the Lord will not allow to perish, even though temporal death should come, but will then receive him into eternal life.' The Wesleyan Wesley and Ellicott press the deeper Jewish reading: the phrase is 'one of the earliest and most definite expressions of a sure belief in an eternal future,' now a favourite inscription on Jewish gravestones — 'Here we have laid the body, trusting the soul is bound up in the bundle of life with the Lord.'

Ellicott lets the verse open a whole vista. He gathers the Talmud's meditations — the souls of the righteous 'hidden beneath God's glorious throne' — and hears in them 'the original of St. John's well-known picture of the souls of them that were slain for the word of God' (Rev. 6:9). Even the contrasting figure of the sling, he suggests, was likely 'suggested by the ever memorable encounter between David and Goliath.' What most moves him is that shepherds and herdsmen in the lonely sheep-farms of Judah 'believed in the glories of the life eternal with God' — a light he traces to Samuel and the prophetic schools. The witnesses divide only over degree: whether the bundle of life speaks first of earthly safety or of heaven. On the substance they are one — that God himself will keep his own as men keep their treasure, and fling their enemies away like a stone from the sling.
30:
Abigail speaks of David's kingship as a settled certainty — when the LORD shall have done to my lord according to all the good that he hath spoken concerning thee, and shall have appointed thee ruler over Israel — and the witnesses fasten on the striking confidence of a country woman who talks of an outlawed captain's throne as though it were already his. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch conclude that her words imply she 'had received certain information of the anointing of David, and his designation to be the future king, probably through Samuel, or one of the pupils of the prophets.' Her whole bearing shows 'such an advance and maturity in the life of faith as could only have been derived from intercourse with prophets.'

Ellicott reads the same certainty and traces it to the same source. She speaks of David's coming reign 'as a matter of absolute certainty,' a thing she 'had doubtless heard through the Prophetic Schools'; for by this time not a few of Samuel's pupils 'had been working as teachers and preachers throughout the land.' He even ventures that some early form of the synagogue — 'some kind of assembly for prayer to the God of Israel for instruction and exhortation' — had already taken root, the 'sons of the prophets' being 'the first Teachers, the first rabbis in Israel,' at a time when the central sanctuary exercised comparatively little influence over the people's religious life. The Reformed Gill keeps to the plainer sense, taking 'all the good that he hath spoken' to mean God's performing his promise 'especially with respect to his kingdom.'

The interest the witnesses share is not a dispute but a discovery: how did a woman on a Judean sheep-farm come by so firm a grasp of David's destiny? Their answer — the prophetic schools Samuel had founded, whose influence the chapter's opening verse had already honoured at his death — quietly ties the two ends of the chapter together. The prophet is buried; his work lives on in the faith of an Abigail. And that faith gives her plea its final leverage: she is not flattering a fugitive with dreams of greatness but reminding the Lord's anointed of what the Lord has surely spoken, so that the very certainty of his throne becomes the reason he must not defile his hands. The commentators do not divide; together they marvel at the ripened piety of the speaker and let it point back to the schools that formed it.
31:
Abigail's argument comes to rest on David's own future conscience — this shall be no grief unto thee, nor offence of heart unto my lord... that thou hast shed blood causeless, or that my lord hath avenged himself: but when the LORD shall have dealt well with my lord, then remember thine handmaid — and the witnesses judge this her most powerful stroke of all. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch parse it as the conclusion of the preceding verse: the forgiveness of Nabal's folly 'will not be a stumbling-block' — a pukah, something in the road that makes a man stagger — 'and anguish of heart' to David when he comes to the throne. Ellicott hears the exquisite grace of it: she winds up her plea 'with a reference to the period when those happy days should have arrived,' urging that David 'must have no deeds of violence, of furious passion, and of shed blood to look back upon,' but rather must 'remember then that Abigail had saved him from the commission of a wild and sinful act.'

The Wesleyan Wesley spells out the appeal to conscience: to shed this blood would leave David's mind never 'free from all the torment which such an action would cause'; the deed would be 'causeless,' for Nabal 'had done nothing worthy of death by the laws of God or of man,' and the rest of the family were innocent; and to avenge himself would be 'directly contrary to God's law' (Lev. 19:18; Deut. 32:35). The Reformed Gill reads the same promise of peace — the act, once done, would sit uneasy on the conscience, but forborne it would give 'no trouble, nor distress of mind.'

The Puritan Matthew Henry notes that she 'reserves this argument for the last, as a very powerful one with so good a man' — that 'the less he indulged his passion the more he consulted his peace and the repose of his own conscience.' He draws the maxim the scene teaches: 'many have done that in a heat which they have a thousand times wished undone again; the sweetness of revenge is soon turned into bitterness.' The witnesses do not contend; together they show a wise woman appealing past David's present fury to the David he will one day be, bidding him spare himself a memory he could never bear — and sealing it with the modest wish that, when God has made him great, he will 'remember thine handmaid.'
32:
David answers with a doxology — Blessed be the LORD God of Israel, which sent thee this day to meet me — and the witnesses hear in it the sound of a soul recalled to itself. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch observe that Abigail's words 'could not fail to appease David's wrath,' and that in his reply he first 'praised the Lord for having sent Abigail to meet him' before turning to congratulate her, since otherwise he would surely have carried out the revenge he had sworn. David begins, that is, not with the instrument but with the hand that wielded it.

The Puritan Matthew Henry makes the exchange a little emblem of the wise reprover and the humble heart. 'As an ear-ring of gold, and an ornament of fine gold, so is a wise reprover upon an obedient ear' (Prov. 25:12); Abigail 'was a wise reprover of David's passion, and he gave an obedient ear to the reproof,' true to his own rule, 'let the righteous smite me, it shall be a kindness' (Ps. 141:5). 'Never was such an admonition either better given or better taken.' Henry draws two lessons from David's opening blessing: that 'God is to be acknowledged in all the kindnesses that our friends do us,' since whoever meets us 'with counsel, direction, comfort, caution, or seasonable reproof, we must see God sending them'; and that 'we ought to be very thankful for those happy providences which are means of preventing sin.'

The Reformed Gill pictures David 'having heard her out, and being overcome by her rhetoric and powerful arguments,' breaking into praise. What strikes the witnesses is the direction of his gratitude. He does not merely thank Abigail; he blesses God for sending her, seeing in her timely coming not a lucky accident but a mercy aimed straight at his imperilled soul. The commentators do not divide; together they mark the greatness of a man who, checked in mid-fury, does not sulk at being thwarted but thanks God for the check — reading his own frustrated revenge as a rescue, and the woman who blocked his path as heaven's messenger. It is, they agree, the surest sign that the true David has returned: he blesses the God who would not let him sin.
33:
David blesses the counsel and the counsellor together — blessed be thy advice, and blessed be thou, which hast kept me this day from coming to shed blood, and from avenging myself with mine own hand — and the witnesses admire the frankness of a great man owning himself in the wrong. The grammatical-historical Ellicott puts it plainly: David, 'with his usual frank generosity, allows he has been in the wrong in giving way to wild, ungovernable passion, and openly confesses that if Abigail had not met him and reasoned with him, he would have carried out his purpose, and stained his fair fame for ever with a terrible crime.' The Reformed Gill notes that 'thy advice' renders a word for 'thy taste' — her 'discretion, prudence, and understanding,' as the Targum has it — so that David blesses precisely the good sense that had saved him.

Ellicott then widens the moment into a reflection on rash vows. It is 'noticeable how, in this age of deeper religion and of higher culture, the old superstitious reverence for vows, taken in moments of frenzy or of extreme excitement, had given place to a calmer and more reasonable spirit.' Never was a more solemn oath sworn than David's that morning; 'yet, before the sun set he is convinced of the wickedness of his purpose, and sooner than carry it out he deliberately breaks the oath.' He sets the contrast starkly against Saul, who all but murdered Jonathan to keep a rash vow (1 Sam. 14:24), and against Jephthah, who 'ruthlessly carried out his wild, useless oath' (Judg. 11:34).

The Puritan Matthew Henry weighs the rarity of such gratitude for reproof. 'Most people think it enough if they take a reproof patiently, but we meet with few that will take it thankfully and will commend those that give it.' Abigail, he notes, did not rejoice more to have saved her household from David's sword than David did to have been saved by her from his own sin — for 'the nearer we are to committing sin the greater is the mercy of a seasonable restraint.' The witnesses do not divide; they hold up David's double blessing as the model response to correction — not resentment at being crossed but thankfulness to the one who crossed him — and let his ready confession teach that the truly great heart is the one that can be stopped, and can bless the hand that stops it.
34:
David seals his gratitude with a second oath, this time in Abigail's favour — as the LORD God of Israel liveth, which hath kept me back from hurting thee, except thou hadst hasted and come to meet me, surely there had not been left unto Nabal by the morning light any that pisseth against the wall — and the witnesses read it as a man measuring, with a shudder, how near he had come to the edge. The Reformed Gill marks the solemnity, taking the words as 'an oath for the confirmation of what he was about to say,' the very form of adjuration now turned from a threat of slaughter into a confession of rescue. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch note the strongly adversative force of David's 'nevertheless': but for her haste, the sworn destruction would have fallen.

The Puritan Matthew Henry dwells on the two things David now sees clearly — the greatness of the sin and the nearness of the fall. He 'speaks of the sin as very great,' for he was 'coming to shed blood,' the very guilt his own prayer dreaded — 'Deliver me from blood-guiltiness' (Ps. 51:14) — and coming, worse, 'to avenge himself with his own hand,' which is 'stepping into the throne of God, who has said, Vengeance is mine; I will repay' (Rom. 12:19). And he speaks of the danger as imminent: 'Except thou hadst hasted, the bloody execution had been done.' Henry draws the lesson that runs through the whole rescue — 'the nearer we were to the commission of sin the greater was the mercy of a seasonable restraint.'

There is a fine detail the witnesses do not miss: David owns that God 'hath kept me back from hurting thee' — hinting, as Henry suggests, that at Abigail's first appearing there may have flickered a thought to strike down even her for daring to block his path, so that he reckons it mercy to have been given patience to hear her out. The verse holds almost no debate; its whole weight is confession. David repeats the very words of his terrible vow — 'any that pisseth against the wall' — not now to swear it but to marvel that it was not fulfilled, and to name the two rescuers plainly: the Lord who withheld him, and the woman who hasted. Together, the commentators agree, they turn a broken oath of blood into a standing testimony that God had kept his servant from the worst.
35:
The scene closes in peace — So David received of her hand that which she had brought him, and said unto her, Go up in peace to thine house; see, I have hearkened to thy voice, and have accepted thy person — and the witnesses linger on the completeness of the reconciliation. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch note that David 'received the gifts brought for him, and bade Abigail return to her house, with the assurance that he had granted her request for pardon,' the phrase 'accepted thy person' being the settled idiom of gracious acceptance (Gen. 19:21). The Wesleyan Wesley glosses the same words: David 'showed my acceptance of thy person, by my grant of thy request.' The Reformed Gill observes that he 'kindly took the present' for himself and his men — 'a present for a prince' — the acceptance of the gift being itself the seal of the peace.

The Puritan Matthew Henry reads the dismissal as David owning himself fairly overcome. He 'does, in effect, own himself vanquished by her eloquence,' saying, 'I have hearkened to thy voice, for I have accepted thy person, am well pleased with thee and what thou hast said.' From this Henry gathers a cluster of truths: that 'wise and good men will hear reason, and let that rule them, though it come from those that are every way their inferiors, and though their passions are up'; that 'oaths cannot bind us to that which is sinful,' for David had solemnly vowed Nabal's death and would have done worse to perform it than he did to swear it; and that 'a wise and faithful reproof is often better taken, and speeds better, than we expected' (Prov. 28:23).

What the witnesses find most telling is the phrase go up in peace. The captain who came down the mountain to shed blood sends the woman back up it with a benediction; the mission that set out to leave no male of Nabal's house alive ends with its mistress dismissed in honour, her plea granted, her gift received, her person accepted. The commentators do not divide; together they mark the double victory of the hour — Abigail's, who has saved her household by wisdom, and David's, greater still, who has conquered himself by hearkening. The peace he pronounces over her is, in truth, the peace restored to his own soul, and the whole tense encounter dissolves in the quiet dignity of a man who has let reason and mercy have their way.
36:
The scene cuts back to Nabal, oblivious at his feast — he held a feast in his house, like the feast of a king; and Nabal's heart was merry within him, for he was very drunken — and the witnesses read the contrast with grim relish. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch picture the returning Abigail finding him at 'a king's feast, very merry and drunken above measure' (Prov. 23:30), so that 'she told him nothing, less or more, until the morning light.' Ellicott takes the tableau as the completing stroke of the portrait: it 'completes the picture of the wealthy sheep-master,' and sets in sharp relief the contrast between 'the churlish, obstinate' husband, 'indulging to excess in the coarse pleasures of the table,' and 'the high-minded and wise Abigail.'

The Puritan Matthew Henry makes the feast a sermon on false security. Abigail came home to find Nabal 'in the midst of his jollity, little thinking how near he was to ruin' — for 'sinners are often most secure when they are most in danger and destruction is at the door.'
