Henry marvels at the man's double folly: extravagant in entertainment, keeping 'a feast like the feast of a king' though his guests were but sheep-shearers, and sottish in appetite, so drunk that 'there is not a surer sign that a man has but little wisdom, nor a surer way to ruin the little he has, than drinking to excess.' To give the drunkard good counsel would be 'to cast pearls before swine' (Matt. 7:6); better to wait, as Abigail wisely did, 'till they are sober.'

The Wesleyan Wesley sharpens the same point: in Nabal 'sordid covetousness and vain prodigality met together' — the man who grudged David water lavishes a royal banquet on himself — and Abigail told him nothing because, drunk, 'he was incapable of admonition, his reason and conscience being both asleep.' The Reformed Gill notes only that she found him 'at a great feast' on her return. The witnesses do not divide; together they frame the chapter's deepest irony — that at the very hour a wise woman was on her knees in the dust to save his life, Nabal sat drinking himself insensible, secure in a plenty he had no grace to use, his ruin already appointed and himself the last to know it.
37:
The morning brings the reckoning — when the wine was gone out of Nabal, and his wife had told him these things, that his heart died within him, and he became as a stone — and the witnesses read it as the sudden collapse of a man forced at last to see his danger. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch take the words to mean that when Nabal 'had become sober' and heard the tale, 'he was so terrified that he was smitten with a stroke,' his heart dying and turning to stone; and they weigh the cause with care — not merely anger at his loss or alarm at the peril, but also 'his vexation that his wife should have made him humble himself,' for he was 'a hard, self-willed man.' The Wesleyan Clarke agrees on the medical fact: he was 'thunderstruck,' the fear of what he had escaped so overcoming his mind that he grew 'insensible to all things around him,' refused nourishment, and lingered ten days.

The one real question is what struck the blow. Ellicott lays out two readings of the apoplexy. It may have been 'brought on by fear,' as Nabal learned 'to what a terrible danger he had been exposed,' realizing that in his drunken sleep he and all his house 'would have perished miserably had it not been for his wife's forethought'; or it may have been 'a furious burst of anger at his wife's intelligence,' unbearable to a man who hated David, that 'she should have humiliated herself' before such a one. Ellicott judges the first 'the more probable,' and Keil's note that vexation played its part keeps the second alive alongside it.

The Puritan Matthew Henry catches the pathos of the change with a phrase the others echo: 'his heart overnight merry with wine, next morning heavy as a stone; so deceitful are carnal pleasures, so transient the laughter of the fool.' The Reformed Gill notes only that Abigail waited until he was sober and 'capable of understanding.' The witnesses divide narrowly — fear or rage as the trigger — but agree on the sight: the churl who had blustered 'Who is David?' and drunk himself into a king's stupor is felled in an instant by the bare knowledge of how near he came to death, his stony silence the first instalment of the judgment the next verse will complete.
38:
Judgment falls in a single sentence — it came to pass about ten days after, that the LORD smote Nabal, that he died — and the witnesses read the brief notice as the plain hand of God completing what the stroke began. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch take it exactly so: 'about ten days later the Lord smote him, so that he died,' the Lord putting 'an end to his life by a second stroke.' The Reformed Gill pictures the interval, the man lying 'ten days' in his stupid and senseless state before the end came.

Over the manner of it there is a delicate difference. Ellicott is careful to keep the natural and the providential together: the death was 'a second apoplectic stroke,' and 'although the death was a sequel to the selfishness, the passion, and the intemperance, it does not appear that anything more than the operation of natural causes occasioned the end.' Yet he grants at once that 'in the language of these old divinely inspired writers, disease and sickness are often spoken of as the special shafts aimed by the Most High, as in fact they are.' The narrator's own phrase — the LORD smote Nabal — leaves no doubt where the sacred writer lays the stroke, whatever the secondary means.

The Puritan Matthew Henry presses the moral without flinching. Nabal, so lately 'wonderfully rescued from the sword of David,' is now dead indeed — for 'the preservations of wicked men are but reservations for some further sorer strokes of divine wrath.' Henry finds it 'just with God that those who live without grace should die without comfort,' and marks that 'here is no lamentation made for Nabal; he departed without being lamented,' a silence the more telling beside the whole nation's weeping over Samuel at the chapter's head. The witnesses do not really divide; whether they name the immediate cause a stroke or simply the blow of God, they agree that Nabal's end was no accident but a recompense — the folly and greed and drunken security of the man ripening, in ten quiet days, into the death the Lord himself is said to send.
39:
Nabal's death draws from David both a doxology and a proposal — Blessed be the LORD, that hath pleaded the cause of my reproach from the hand of Nabal, and hath kept his servant from evil... And David sent and communed with Abigail, to take her to him to wife — and the witnesses read the two acts together. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch note that David 'praised Jehovah that He had avenged his shame upon Nabal, and held him back from self-revenge,' the Lord himself having 'pleaded the cause of my reproach' where David had been forbidden to plead it with his own sword. The Puritan Matthew Henry unfolds the thanksgiving into three strands: David blesses God that he was 'kept from killing him,' rejoicing that Nabal 'died a natural death and not by his hand'; that God had 'vindicated David's honour'; and that the whole affair encouraged him and others 'to commit their cause to God, when they are in any way injured,' assured that in his own time he will redress their wrongs.

Not all the witnesses are content with David's blessing. The Wesleyan Wesley hears in it 'another instance of human infirmity in David' — a satisfaction at an enemy's death not wholly free from the passion Abigail had just quieted — and adds the sober chronological note that the marriage 'was not done immediately after Nabal's death, but some time after it.' The Wesleyan Clarke turns to the second act, observing that David had likely heard how Saul had married off Michal to another, which 'justified David in taking Abigail,' and that by the match 'he became possessed of all Nabal's property in Carmel and Maon.'

The Reformed Gill notes only that David 'soon' heard the news, Maon and Carmel lying near the wilderness. The debate the witnesses raise is real but gentle: whether David's blessing of God at Nabal's death is pure and proper thanksgiving (Henry, Keil) or carries a trace of the very vindictiveness he had renounced (Wesley). Yet all agree on the providence they see displayed — that the vengeance David was mercifully prevented from taking, God took himself; that the wrong left in the Lord's hands was righted without a drop of blood on the future king; and that the wise woman who had saved David from murder now becomes, by the same providence, his wife.
40:
David's messengers carry the proposal to Carmel — David sent us unto thee, to take thee to him to wife — and the witnesses attend both to the swiftness of Abigail's consent and to the wider meaning some of them find folded in the union. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch report that when the servants conveyed David's wish, she consented 'without hesitation,' answering 'with deep reverence' that his handmaid was but 'a servant to wash the feet of the servants of my lord' — the obsequious style of the East, meaning 'I am ready to perform the humblest possible service for thee.' The Reformed Gill notes only that she was found at Carmel, where perhaps she 'chose to be after his death, rather than at Maon.'

Ellicott pauses over the timing, observing that the interval between Nabal's death and this mission 'is not specified'; the legal mourning was but seven days, yet 'a very considerable period may have elapsed.' He then records the reading of the Latin father Ambrose of Milan, who — 'as is usual in Patristic expositions' — allegorises the marriage, comparing 'the espousals of Abigail to David after Nabal's death to the union of the Church,' that is, the Gentile world, 'to Christ after the cessation of its connection with heathenism.' It is a solitary voice from the older, figurative school, set beside the plain historical reading of the rest, and it lifts the domestic scene toward the great marriage of the Lamb.

What the witnesses share is admiration for the woman who answers a king's suit by offering to wash his servants' feet. The very humility that had won David on the mountain now marks her acceptance of his hand: she who had flung herself in the dust to save her household bows again, in reverence, to become its future queen. The commentators do not contend over the plain sense; the only outlier is Ambrose's mystical turn, and even that grows out of what all of them feel — that this is no ordinary match. In the union of the wise and lowly Abigail with the anointed but still outlawed David, the story quietly binds together the two figures the chapter has most honoured, and, for at least one ancient reader, foreshadows a greater bridegroom and a greater bride.
41:
Abigail's answer is all reverence — she arose, and bowed herself on her face to the earth, and said, Behold, let thine handmaid be a servant to wash the feet of the servants of my lord — and the witnesses dwell on the depth of the humility a great lady shows to a king's messengers. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch render the words as 'deep reverence,' the 'obsequious style of the East' by which she declares herself ready for the lowliest office of all — not merely to serve David, but to wash the feet of the very servants who bring his suit. The Reformed Gill notes the significance of the gesture: she 'bowed herself on her face to the earth' as she had done before David himself, treating his messengers 'as representing him,' and so paying them 'the same respect and reverence, and did the same honour, as if he had been there in person.'

Feet-washing was the servant's task, the meanest courtesy of an Eastern house, and Abigail reaches for that very image to measure her sense of the honour done her. She does not answer a proposal of marriage with the composure of a wealthy widow weighing her worth; she answers it as one lifted far above her deserving, unworthy even to stoop among the lowest of David's household. The grammatical-historical witnesses add that, having so spoken, she 'rose up hastily' to follow the messengers — the same readiness that had marked her generosity and her intercession now carrying her without delay to the man she believed the Lord had chosen.

The witnesses find no dispute here, only a consistency worth marking. The woman introduced at the outset as 'of good understanding' has shown that understanding at every turn — in the swift present, the disarming plea, the prophecy of David's throne — and now shows it in the manner of her consent. Her lowliness is not weakness but wisdom; she who had knelt to save her house kneels again to enter David's, and the reverence she pays his messengers is the same reverence she had paid the Lord's anointed on the mountain. The commentators let the bow speak for itself: the future queen of Israel first appears as one content to wash the feet of servants, and it is precisely such self-abasement, the whole chapter has taught, that God delights to raise.
42:
Abigail's consent becomes her journey — Abigail hasted, and arose, and rode upon an ass, with five damsels of hers that went after her; and she went after the messengers of David, and became his wife — and the witnesses read her promptness as the fruit of faith. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch mark the swiftness plainly: she 'rose up hastily, and went after the messengers to David with five damsels in her train, and became his wife,' the retinue befitting her rank. The Reformed Gill draws out the settled mind behind the haste: she had 'no objection nor hesitation,' holding David in as high esteem as he held her; and though 'she was rich and he poor, this was no obstacle in the way,' for 'she knew and believed he would be king of Israel,' and had enough of her own 'to support herself, and supply him, till he came to the throne.'

The Puritan Matthew Henry reads the marriage as an act of faith and turns it toward the Gospel. Abigail 'did not upbraid him with his present distresses, nor ask him how he could maintain her,' but valued him because 'she knew he was a very good man' and 'believed he would, in due time, be a very great man.' 'She married him in faith,' Henry says, 'not questioning but that, though now he had not a house of his own that he durst bring her to, yet God's promise to him would at length be fulfilled.' From this he draws the analogy that lifts the scene: 'thus those who join themselves to Christ must be willing now to suffer with him, believing that hereafter they shall reign with him.'

What the witnesses honour is the courage of a woman who weds a hunted outlaw on the strength of a promise not yet kept. To marry David then was to embrace his wilderness and his danger, to leave the security of Nabal's estate for the uncertain fortunes of a man with a price on his life; and she does it 'hastily,' without haggling or hesitation, staking her future on the word of God concerning him. The commentators raise no dispute; together they set her faith beside her wisdom as the two graces that make her the fit partner of the coming king. The chapter that began by burying Israel's prophet ends by joining to the anointed shepherd a wife who believed, before the crown was his, that God would surely give it — and who was willing to share his exile until he did.
43:
A brief notice on David's marriages opens here — David also took Ahinoam of Jezreel; and they were also both of them his wives — and the witnesses use it to fix a place, an order, and a warning. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch identify the town: not the Jezreel of Issachar (Josh. 19:18) but the one 'in the mountains of Judah' (Josh. 15:56), near Maon; and they read the 'also' as pointing back to David's earlier marriage to Michal. The Wesleyan Clarke thinks Ahinoam was married 'before Abigail,' since 'she is always mentioned first in the list of his wives' and was the mother of David's eldest son Amnon. The Reformed Gill reads the verse the same way, taking Ahinoam to have become his wife 'before Abigail.'

The grammatical-historical Ellicott and the Puritan Matthew Henry turn the plural marriages into a sober reflection. Ellicott names 'the fatal results of this disastrous and unhappy Oriental custom of polygamy,' which showed themselves 'as time went on' in David's household — 'a plentiful crop of intrigues, crimes, and murders in the royal palace' — 'one of the curses of the East.' Henry grants that 'David was carried away by the corrupt custom of those times,' but insists 'from the beginning it was not so' (Matt. 19:4-5), and suggests that 'Saul's defrauding David of his only rightful wife' — the theft of Michal to be told in the next verse — 'was the occasion of his running into this irregularity,' for 'when the knot of conjugal affection is once loosed, it is scarcely ever tied fast again.'

The witnesses divide only on the small matter of chronology — whether Ahinoam or Abigail was married first — and agree that the text names them together as 'both his wives.' On the larger matter they are of one mind, and unusually candid about their hero. They will not gloss David's polygamy as innocent custom; Henry measures it against the Creator's first ordinance, and Ellicott against its own bitter harvest in the king's later griefs. The verse thus stands as a quiet blemish set deliberately in the record beside Abigail's praise — a reminder that even the man after God's own heart bent to the fashion of his age, and that the household so begun would breed the sorrows the books of Samuel go on to tell.
44:
The chapter closes on a marriage broken to match the two just made — But Saul had given Michal his daughter, David's wife, to Phalti the son of Laish, which was of Gallim — and the witnesses read Saul's act as the deliberate sundering of the tie between king and outlaw. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch record the plain facts and their sequel: Saul 'had taken his daughter Michal away from David, and given her to Palti of Gallim' — the Paltiel of a later chapter (2 Sam. 3:15) — a town between Gibeah and Jerusalem (Isa. 10:30); and they note that 'after the death of Saul, David persuaded Ishbosheth to give him Michal back again' (2 Sam. 3:14). The Wesleyan Clarke adds that Phalti was probably a man of Saul's own tribe, chosen for that reason.

The motive is where the witnesses differ in emphasis. The Reformed Gill sees a threefold spite: Saul gave Michal to another 'partly to vex David,' partly to break the relation of son-in-law that might restrain his persecution, and partly lest the marriage should 'give David any show of claim' or help him 'to the throne.' The grammatical-historical Ellicott stresses the political calculation — the act 'showed on the part of Saul a fixed determination to break utterly and for ever with David,' Phalti being 'a chieftain whom Saul was desirous of attracting to his fortunes' — and recalls that Michal had once 'loved David' (1 Sam. 18:20), following her story on to her later restoration by Abner and her sad estrangement in the day of the ark (2 Sam. 6:20-23).

The Puritan Matthew Henry reads it as one more wound in David's tangled domestic life: Michal, 'the wife of his youth, to whom he would have been constant,' was given away 'in token of Saul's displeasure,' the king thereby 'disclaiming the relation of a father-in-law.' The witnesses do not really divide; whether they weigh chiefly Saul's malice (Gill), his cold policy (Ellicott), or the personal grief of it (Henry), they agree that the verse sets a broken bond beside two new ones, and closes the chapter on a note of unfinished business. Samuel is buried, Nabal is judged, Abigail is won — but Michal is torn away, and the reader is left to feel how much of David's story, and of Saul's ruin, still hangs unresolved as the book moves on.
